question.question: Based on the detonation report for this MSIL.Bladabindi sample, which of the following behaviours constitute Defense Evasion techniques? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. The malware allocates memory regions with PAGE_GUARD access protection to hinder memory dumping
B. The malware calls GetAsyncKeyState to capture pressed keystrokes
C. The malware uses System.IO.Compression (GZipStream) and System.Reflection.Assembly.Load to unpack and reflectively load code at runtime
D. The malware spawns netsh.exe to add a firewall allowedprogram rule for its own executable
E. The malware calls GetSystemDefaultLangID to retrieve the default system language
F. The malware calls GetLocaleInfoW to retrieve locale information
G. The malware writes 32 bytes to a remote process (WriteProcessMemory) to inject code

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following observed behaviours or indicators are specifically attributable to Defense Evasion tactics — and NOT to Discovery or other unrelated tactics?
options: A. The PE binary is packed with MPRESS packer, with a high-entropy section (.MPRESS1) and entrypoint located in an uncommon section (.MPRESS2)
B. microsofthelp.exe called CreateToolhelp32Snapshot to enumerate running processes
C. hash.exe wrote data to a remote process (WriteProcessMemory), consistent with process injection
D. hash.exe read registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CONTROLSET001\CONTROL to retrieve system language information
E. microsofthelp.exe marked C:\hash.exe for deletion using file deletion access rights
F. microsofthelp.exe slept 525 times across its execution to evade time-based sandbox analysis
G. microsofthelp.exe called GetVersionExA to retrieve operating system version information
H. hash.exe dropped microsofthelp.exe into the Windows system directory, masquerading as a legitimate system binary

question.question: Reviewing the detonation report signatures and MITRE ATT&CK mappings, which of the following observed behaviours specifically constitute Defense Evasion techniques? (Select ALL that apply)
options: A. UPX packing with a zero-size UPX0 section and entrypoint in UPX1 (T1027.002)
B. Use of GetSystemTimeAsFileTime and QueryPerformanceCounter for time-based sandbox evasion (T1497.003)
C. Use of GetLocaleInfoW and GetUserDefaultUILanguage to query system locale and language (T1614 / T1614.001)
D. WriteProcessMemory and RtlCreateUserThread observed in API stream for process injection (T1055 / T1055.001)
E. Timestomping via GetSystemTimeAsFileTime reference mapped to T1070.006
F. Dynamic API resolution via GetProcAddress and LoadLibraryA to conceal imports (T1027.007)
G. Screen capture capability via GetDC API reference (T1113)
H. Execution Guardrails indicators observed in combined indicator analysis (T1480)

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following behaviours constitute Defense Evasion techniques employed by this malware? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. Spawning processes with names matching legitimate Windows system processes (e.g., WINLOGON.EXE, CSRSS.EXE, LSASS.EXE) from non-standard paths under %APPDATA%\WINDOWS\ (T1036.005)
B. Calling GetSystemDefaultLangID and GetLocaleInfoW to determine the system's default language settings (T1614.001)
C. Hooking file system APIs such as FindFirstFileW@KERNEL32.DLL within IExplorer.exe (T1056.004)
D. Calling CreateToolhelp32Snapshot and Process32FirstW to enumerate running processes (T1057)
E. Marking files for deletion after execution to remove traces from disk (T1070.004)
F. Writing a PE file header to disk from hash.exe during execution (T1105)
G. Calling FileTimeToLocalFileTime to query and manipulate file timestamps (T1070.006)
H. Calling GetVersionExA and RtlGetVersion to retrieve operating system version information (T1082)
I. Dropping executable files into the %APPDATA% directory using names that masquerade as Windows system binaries (T1036)

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following behaviours constitute Defense Evasion techniques — as opposed to Discovery or other tactics — directly evidenced by signatures or API calls in the report?
options: A. The sample was packed with PECompact 2.x, indicating Software Packing (T1027.002)
B. The sample calls GetLocaleInfoW to retrieve local language information, indicating System Location Discovery (T1614)
C. The sample's .text section has entropy of ~7.99, indicating Embedded Payloads / high-entropy obfuscation (T1027.009)
D. The sample calls CreateToolhelp32Snapshot to enumerate running processes, indicating Process Discovery (T1057)
E. The sample marks files for deletion via cmd.exe executing a batch script, indicating Indicator Removal: File Deletion (T1070.004)
F. The sample modifies proxy settings by deleting a registry value under HKCU, indicating Modify Registry for Defense Evasion (T1112)
G. The sample calls GetForegroundWindow to retrieve the foreground window handle, indicating Application Window Discovery (T1010)
H. The sample writes data to a remote process, indicating Process Injection used for Defense Evasion (T1055)

question.question: Based on the detonation report for Worm.Ludbaruma, which of the following observed behaviours are specifically attributable to Defense Evasion techniques (as opposed to Discovery, Persistence, or other tactics)? Select ALL options that represent Defense Evasion and ONLY Defense Evasion.
options: A. Creating fake system processes (WINLOGON.EXE, CSRSS.EXE, LSASS.EXE) placed in %APPDATA%\WINDOWS\ instead of their legitimate system paths — Masquerading (T1036.005)
B. Calling CreateToolhelp32Snapshot and Process32FirstW to enumerate running processes — Process Discovery (T1057)
C. Disabling Windows Registry Editor (regedit) via HKCU\SOFTWARE\MICROSOFT\WINDOWS registry key modification — Modify Registry (T1112)
D. Calling GetSystemDefaultLangID and GetLocaleInfoW to retrieve the system's default language settings — System Language Discovery (T1614.001)
E. Writing data to a remote process via VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory — Process Injection (T1055)
F. Marking files for deletion (e.g., C:\share\XK\New Folder.exe) to remove indicators — File Deletion (T1070.004)
G. Calling FileTimeToLocalFileTime in context of file timestamp manipulation — Timestomp (T1070.006)
H. Calling GetVersionExA and RtlGetVersion to query the OS version — System Information Discovery (T1082)
I. Changing file extension appearance in Explorer via HKCU\SOFTWARE registry modification — Change Default File Association (T1546.001)

options: A. The binary contains a high-entropy .text section, indicating software packing or embedded payload obfuscation (T1027.009)
B. The process queries registry keys under HKLM\SYSTEM, which is consistent with System Information Discovery

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following behaviours constitute Defense Evasion techniques specifically attributed to this sample? Select ALL options that are directly evidenced as Defense Evasion in the report.
options: A. The sample sleeps for an abnormally long duration (~1097794852 ms) to delay or evade sandbox analysis (T1497.003)
B. The sample calls GetLocaleInfoA to retrieve the system locale, mapped to System Location Discovery (T1614)
C. The sample uses NtQueueApcThread to queue an Asynchronous Procedure Call into a remote process (T1055.004)
D. The sample calls IsWow64Process to determine whether it is running under WOW64 emulation (T1057)
E. The sample carries a high-entropy .text section consistent with packed or embedded payload content (T1027.009)
F. The sample performs timestomping as indicated by the Timestomp technique mapped under Defense Evasion (T1070.006)
G. The sample calls RtlGetVersion to retrieve OS version information, mapped to System Information Discovery (T1082)
H. The sample disables or modifies security tools as indicated by the Disable or Modify Tools technique (T1562.001)

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following behaviours constitute Defense Evasion techniques used by this malware? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. The binary is packed with UPX, with a high-entropy section (UPX1) and a zero-size UPX0 section
B. The malware calls CreateToolhelp32Snapshot to enumerate running processes
C. The malware writes data to a remote process using cross-process memory write APIs
D. The malware calls RtlGetVersion to retrieve operating system version information
E. The malware uses dynamic API resolution to look up procedure addresses at runtime
F. The malware calls FindFirstFileW to search directories for files
G. The malware drops and executes additional PE files, embedding payloads within itself
H. The malware calls IsWow64Process to check whether it is running under WOW64

question.question: Based on the detonation report, which of the following observations specifically support a Defense Evasion classification rather than Discovery or another tactic? Select ALL that apply.
options: A. The binary is packed with UPX (UPX0/UPX1 sections detected, high-entropy UPX1 section with entropy ~7.97), obfuscating the payload from static analysis
B. 'hash.exe' called CreateToolhelp32Snapshot to enumerate running processes
C. 'hash.exe' wrote a PE file header to disk and dropped additional executable files, consistent with embedded payload staging
D. '0260006.exe' called IsWow64Process to determine the execution environment
E. Dynamic API resolution is observed via GetProcAddress-style calls to retrieve function addresses at runtime, avoiding static import visibility
F. 'hash.exe' called RtlGetVersion to retrieve system version information
G. The sample uses User Activity Based Checks (T1497.002) and Time Based Evasion (T1497.003) to delay or conditionally execute malicious behaviour
H. 'hash.exe' wrote 32 bytes to a remote process, indicating cross-process memory writes
